stem: You are the GP seeing a 64-year-old woman 12 years post natural menopause complaining of urinary frequency, dysuria with sterile MSUs, recurrent post-coital spotting and superficial dyspareunia. She has tried over-the-counter vaginal moisturisers without benefit. She has a history of stage I oestrogen-receptor positive breast cancer treated 6 years ago with mastectomy and tamoxifen; tamoxifen ceased 4 years ago. She has no abnormal uterine bleeding on systematic enquiry, is not on systemic HRT, and her last cervical screening was negative HPV.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic pale vulval and vaginal mucosa, minor friable areas at the introitus, no visible lesions, no abnormal bleeding on speculum, no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: MSU sterile on three occasions, no haematuria on dipstick, no postmenopausal bleeding

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Continue lubricants and moisturisers alone and reassess in 12 months at the next visit
options.rationale: She has already failed over-the-counter moisturisers and lubricants, with recurrent urinary symptoms and dyspareunia significantly affecting quality of life. (Source: AMS)

options.text: Refer for vaginal CO2 laser therapy as the first-line non-hormonal treatment option here
options.rationale: Vaginal energy devices have insufficient evidence to recommend as first-line therapy in AU, and topical hormonal options are preferred where appropriate. (Source: TGA position statement)
source_refs: TGA position statement on vaginal energy devices

options.text: Commence oral ospemifene 60 mg daily and outpatient breast-cancer survivorship review
options.rationale: Ospemifene is a SERM contraindicated in women with active or prior oestrogen-dependent breast cancer in Australian product information. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence systemic combined HRT with oestradiol patch and oral micronised progesterone capsule
options.rationale: Systemic HRT is contraindicated in women with a personal history of oestrogen-receptor positive breast cancer because of unacceptable recurrence risk. (Source: AMS)

options.text: Trial low-dose vaginal oestrogen after MDT agreement and informed consent counselling
options.rationale: Local low-dose vaginal oestrogen has minimal systemic absorption and is considered acceptable for genitourinary syndrome of menopause in women with prior hormone-sensitive breast cancer after multidisciplinary discussion, when non-hormonal therapy has failed. (Source: AMS Vaginal symptoms after breast cancer)

explanation.summary: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause causes vulvovaginal atrophy, recurrent urinary symptoms with sterile MSU, dyspareunia and post-coital spotting. Non-hormonal moisturisers are first-line. In women with prior hormone-sensitive breast cancer who fail non-hormonal therapy, low-dose vaginal oestrogen is considered acceptable after informed consent and discussion with the treating oncology team because systemic absorption is minimal. Systemic HRT and ospemifene remain contraindicated.
key_points: Moisturisers and lubricants first, low-dose vaginal oestrogen second if needed.
Vaginal oestrogen acceptable in prior hormone-sensitive breast cancer after MDT discussion.
Systemic HRT and ospemifene contraindicated.
explanation.pearls: Treat genitourinary syndrome in breast cancer survivors stepwise: moisturisers, then lubricants, then low-dose vaginal oestrogen after oncology agreement - not the laser as a first step.

sources.label: TGA position statement on vaginal energy devices

stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar seeing a 30-year-old G2P1 woman at 18 weeks of pregnancy. She has a thin white-grey vaginal discharge with a fishy odour and no itch. She had a preterm birth at 32 weeks in her last pregnancy. Vaginal pH is 5.5 and the wet film shows clue cells. Chlamydia and gonorrhoea PCR are negative, and she has no genital herpes lesions. She is not in labour and the cervix is closed and long on speculum.

data_table.Examination: Thin homogeneous grey discharge with positive whiff test, no cervical motion tenderness, closed cervix
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.5, clue cells on wet film, negative for trichomonas, NAAT negative for chlamydia and gonorrhoea

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Single 2 g oral dose of metronidazole on day one with no follow-up dose afterwards
options.rationale: The single 2 g dose has lower long-term cure rates in pregnancy and is not recommended; a 5 to 7 day course is preferred. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for a 5 to 7 day course in symptomatic pregnancy
options.rationale: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy, especially in women with a prior preterm birth, should be treated with a course of oral metronidazole; treatment in this group has been associated with reduced preterm birth in the AU sexual health and STI guidelines. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Defer all treatment until after delivery because BV is self-limiting in pregnancy here
options.rationale: In a symptomatic pregnant woman with a previous preterm birth, deferring treatment misses an opportunity to reduce preterm-birth recurrence. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Topical clindamycin cream 2% intravaginally for seven nights with abstinence advice
options.rationale: Topical clindamycin cream is an alternative but oral metronidazole remains first-line in pregnancy because it has the strongest evidence in the women-at-risk-of-preterm-birth subgroup; the cream weakens latex condoms and diaphragms. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g as a single dose for empirical coverage of polymicrobial vaginitis
options.rationale: Azithromycin is not effective against the polymicrobial anaerobes of BV; it covers chlamydia, which has already been excluded. (Source: Australian STI Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth, late miscarriage and postpartum endometritis. Symptomatic women, particularly with a prior preterm birth, should be treated; oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 5 to 7 days is first-line in Australia. Topical clindamycin is an alternative. Treatment is not generally indicated in asymptomatic low-risk women.
key_points: Oral metronidazole 400 mg BD 5-7 days first-line in pregnancy.
Single 2 g dose has lower cure rates and is not recommended.
Treat symptomatic women, especially with prior preterm birth.
explanation.pearls: Re-test for BV in pregnancy if symptoms recur; repeated BV plus prior preterm birth is an indication for cervical-length surveillance and consideration of vaginal progesterone.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Reducing risk of preterm birth

subtopic_detail: High-dose folate in women on anti-epileptic medication
stem: You are the GP seeing a 28-year-old woman with focal-onset epilepsy on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily who is planning pregnancy. She has been seizure-free for 14 months. She has no family history of neural tube defects, takes no other medications, and is otherwise well. She is asking what folate dose to take and for how long before conceiving.

data_table.Investigations: Recent serum lamotrigine within target, folate and B12 normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate folate advice?

options.text: Defer folic acid supplementation until the lamotrigine therapy is changed at preconception
options.rationale: Switching seizure medication in a seizure-free woman during preconception is not indicated; folate at the higher dose is the appropriate response. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily started at pregnancy confirmation and continued through to 28 weeks
options.rationale: Starting at confirmation of pregnancy misses the neural-tube closure window (days 21 to 28 post-conception), which is the period folate must already be at therapeutic level. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Folic acid 0.5 mg daily from at least one month preconception until 12 completed weeks
options.rationale: 0.5 mg daily is the standard preconception dose for low-risk women; anti-epileptic medication moves her into the high-dose 5 mg group. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Cease folic acid and replace with multivitamin containing iodine 150 micrograms alone
options.rationale: Iodine supplementation is recommended but does not replace folate; both are needed in pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily from at least one month preconception until 12 completed weeks
options.rationale: Women on anti-epileptic medication are in the high-dose folate group (along with personal or partner history of NTD, type 1 or 2 diabetes pre-pregnancy, BMI over 30 and certain malabsorption disorders) and require 5 mg daily for at least one month preconception and through the first trimester. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception care and Therapeutic Guidelines)

explanation.summary: High-dose folic acid 5 mg daily preconception and through the first 12 weeks of pregnancy is recommended in women on anti-epileptic medication, with diabetes, with BMI over 30, with a personal or partner history of neural tube defect, and with certain malabsorptive conditions. Standard low-risk women take 0.5 mg daily over the same time frame. Iodine 150 micrograms daily is also recommended throughout pregnancy and lactation.
key_points: 5 mg daily preconception and to 12 weeks for high-risk women.
Anti-epileptic medication is a high-risk indication.
Iodine supplementation 150 micrograms daily is separate and additional.
explanation.pearls: Begin 5 mg folate at least 1 month before conception in any woman on anti-epileptic medication; the neural tube has closed by the time pregnancy is suspected on a urine test.

stem: You are the gynaecology registrar in EPAU reviewing a 31-year-old G2P1 woman with right iliac fossa pain and a tubal ectopic pregnancy confirmed on transvaginal ultrasound. There is no haemoperitoneum and the patient is haemodynamically stable. The ectopic mass measures 28 mm and there is no fetal cardiac activity. Her beta-hCG today is 3200 IU/L, full blood count, LFTs, and renal function are normal. She has no current breastfeeding, no pelvic inflammatory features, and is happy to attend for repeated follow-up.

data_table.Examination: Mild right iliac fossa tenderness without peritonism, soft abdomen, no cervical excitation
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 3200 IU/L, Hb 124 g/L, LFTs and UEC normal; TVS: 28 mm right adnexal mass with no yolk sac, no free fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management option to offer?

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG until levels fall below 5 IU/L
options.rationale: Expectant management is appropriate when beta-hCG is below 1000 to 1500 IU/L with a falling trend in a clinically stable woman; at 3200 IU/L there is a higher risk of rupture without intervention. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy of the affected tube under general anaesthetic today
options.rationale: Surgery is preferred when the contralateral tube is damaged, fetal cardiac activity is present, beta-hCG is over 5000 IU/L, mass is over 35 mm, or there are signs of rupture; none of these apply here. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with day 4 and day 7 serum beta-hCG follow up
options.rationale: Tubal ectopic pregnancy with beta-hCG under 5000 IU/L, no fetal heart activity, mass under 35 mm, and haemodynamic stability meets the criteria for single-dose IM methotrexate at 50 mg/m2 with day 4 and 7 beta-hCG to confirm a 15% drop. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal ectopic pregnancy)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingostomy with conservation of the affected tube under GA today
options.rationale: Salpingostomy is considered when the contralateral tube is absent or damaged; it has higher persistent ectopic and recurrence rates than salpingectomy when the other tube is healthy. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Multi-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 every 48 hours with alternate-day folinic acid
options.rationale: The multi-dose regimen is reserved for sites that are difficult to manage with single dose (interstitial, cervical, scar pregnancies) or in trophoblastic disease; single dose is standard for uncomplicated tubal ectopic. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Medical management of tubal ectopic pregnancy with single-dose IM methotrexate is offered when beta-hCG is below 5000 IU/L, the ectopic mass is under 35 mm, no fetal cardiac activity is seen, the patient is clinically stable and can attend for serial beta-hCG follow-up. Folinic acid rescue is not part of the single-dose regimen. Anti-D is given to Rh-negative women. Patients are counselled to avoid pregnancy for 3 months after methotrexate, given teratogenic risk.
key_points: Single dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with day 4 and 7 hCG.
Eligibility: beta-hCG under 5000, mass under 35 mm, no FHR, stable.
Avoid conception for 3 months after methotrexate.
explanation.pearls: If day 4 to 7 beta-hCG falls by less than 15%, give a second methotrexate dose - do not jump to surgery unless rupture or instability.

stem: You are the maternal fetal medicine consultant reviewing a 36-year-old G4P3 woman at 28 weeks gestation with two previous caesarean sections. The current placenta is anterior and covers the lower segment. Greyscale and colour Doppler ultrasound shows loss of the retroplacental clear zone, multiple lacunae and bridging vessels invading the bladder wall. She has no current bleeding. She lives 20 minutes from a tertiary maternity service with onsite interventional radiology and gynae-oncology.

data_table.Examination: Singleton cephalic presentation, fundal height appropriate for dates, no bleeding, no tenderness
data_table.Investigations: US 28 weeks: anterior low placenta with loss of clear zone, lacunae, bridging vessels; MRI confirms suspected placenta percreta with bladder involvement

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Perform amniocentesis at 28 weeks for fetal lung maturity before timing delivery plan
options.rationale: Amniocentesis for lung maturity is not part of AU practice; corticosteroids before planned 34 to 35+6 week delivery is the approach. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Plan a trial of vaginal birth after caesarean given the two previous lower segment LSCS
options.rationale: VBAC is contraindicated by both placenta praevia and suspected accreta; she requires planned caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Planned classical caesarean and caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 35+6 weeks with MDT
options.rationale: RANZCOG and AU placenta accreta spectrum guidance recommends planned delivery at 34 to 35+6 weeks by classical (fundal) caesarean with caesarean hysterectomy by an experienced MDT, after a course of antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta accreta spectrum)

options.text: Admit for inpatient maternal monitoring from 28 weeks until spontaneous labour onset
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is considered only with bleeding episodes or other risk markers; routine admission from 28 weeks is not indicated and consumes bed days without improving outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Plan elective lower segment caesarean section at 39 weeks with attempted placental delivery
options.rationale: Awaiting 39 weeks risks emergency haemorrhage as the lower segment thins and contractions begin; delivery is timed at 34 to 35+6 weeks in PAS. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (accreta, increta, percreta) requires antenatal imaging diagnosis, multidisciplinary planning at a tertiary centre, antenatal corticosteroids and planned classical caesarean with caesarean hysterectomy at 34 to 35+6 weeks. Surgical strategy may include preoperative ureteric stents, cell salvage, interventional radiology balloon catheters and gynae-oncology involvement. Awaiting term risks emergency intrapartum haemorrhage as the lower segment thins.
key_points: Plan delivery 34 to 35+6 weeks with corticosteroid cover.
Classical caesarean and caesarean hysterectomy with MDT.
Avoid placental separation; leaving placenta in situ is associated with higher complication.
explanation.pearls: Antenatally suspected placenta percreta with bladder involvement warrants urology in the operating room and pre-op ureteric stents.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G3P0 woman at 18+2 weeks of pregnancy in a high-risk antenatal clinic. Her last pregnancy ended at 21 weeks with painless cervical dilation and miscarriage, and her first pregnancy ended in a similar second-trimester loss at 22 weeks. She has no current bleeding, contractions or rupture of membranes. Transvaginal ultrasound today shows a closed external os with cervical length 22 mm and no funnelling. She has no STI infection and no other risk factors.

data_table.Examination: Cervix closed externally on speculum, no bleeding, no leaking
data_table.Investigations: TVS cervical length 22 mm, no funnelling, normal fetal anatomy, negative high-vaginal swab for chlamydia and gonorrhoea

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.text: Vaginal progesterone alone until 36 weeks of gestation without any cervical cerclage now
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is appropriate as an adjunct or for short-cervix prevention, but in this woman with a clear history of cervical insufficiency, cerclage is the primary intervention. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: History-indicated McDonald cervical cerclage now plus vaginal progesterone until 36 weeks
options.rationale: Two or more prior second-trimester losses with painless cervical dilation is a history-based indication for cerclage; with cervical shortening already at 22 mm, cerclage is offered and vaginal progesterone supports the obstetric history. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical insufficiency)

options.text: Wait for cervical length to fall below 15 mm before any cerclage or progesterone start
options.rationale: Awaiting further shortening loses the benefit of a planned history-indicated cerclage and risks repeat second-trimester loss. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Place an Arabin pessary and arrange serial cervical length surveillance every two weeks
options.rationale: Pessaries have inconsistent evidence and are not first-line in a woman with two prior second-trimester losses. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Bed rest at home and weekly cervical length scans without any other obstetric intervention
options.rationale: Bed rest has no evidence to prevent preterm birth and increases VTE and deconditioning risk. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: History-indicated cerclage is offered to women with two or more prior second-trimester losses or preterm births consistent with cervical insufficiency, ideally placed at 12 to 14 weeks gestation. Ultrasound-indicated cerclage is offered to women with one prior preterm birth and cervical length under 25 mm before 24 weeks. Physical-exam (rescue) cerclage is offered for cervical dilation in the absence of labour. Vaginal progesterone is an evidence-based adjunct.
key_points: Two or more second-trimester losses = history-indicated cerclage 12-14 weeks.
One prior PTB + CL under 25 mm = ultrasound-indicated cerclage.
Vaginal progesterone supports cerclage; bed rest is not evidence based.
explanation.pearls: When in doubt, transvaginal scan every 2 weeks from 16 to 24 weeks lets you catch ultrasound-indicated cerclage opportunities in moderate-risk women.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Reducing risk of preterm birth

stem: You are the obstetric medicine registrar reviewing a 31-year-old G2P0 woman with established SLE at her booking visit at 8 weeks of pregnancy. She has been clinically well for the last 18 months on hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and low-dose prednisolone 5 mg daily. She has no renal involvement, no antiphospholipid antibodies, and is anti-Ro and anti-La negative. She wants to know whether her medications should be changed and how her care plan should look.

data_table.Examination: No active rash, no joint swelling, normal cardiac and respiratory examination
data_table.Investigations: Stable mild anaemia, normal renal function, urine PCR 5 mg/mmol, low-titre ANA, no APL antibodies

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Cease low-dose prednisolone because of gestational diabetes and fetal growth risk
options.rationale: Low-dose prednisolone (under 20 mg daily) is compatible with pregnancy in active disease; stopping risks flare. (Source: ACR)
source_refs: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases

options.text: Continue hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone, add aspirin from 12 weeks, joint MDT review
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is continued in pregnancy because it reduces flare risk and is safe; low-dose aspirin from 12 weeks is recommended in SLE for pre-eclampsia prevention; pregnancy in stable SLE benefits from joint specialist care. (Source: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases and RANZCOG)
source_refs: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases

options.text: Switch hydroxychloroquine to methotrexate for better disease activity control now
options.rationale: Methotrexate is teratogenic and must be ceased before conception with a washout period. (Source: ACR)
source_refs: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases

options.text: Start enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for VTE and pre-eclampsia prevention
options.rationale: LMWH is reserved for women with antiphospholipid syndrome or other VTE risk factors; pre-eclampsia prevention in SLE without APL is by aspirin, not LMWH. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy)

options.text: Cease hydroxychloroquine because of teratogenic concern and continue prednisolone alone
options.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is one of the safer rheumatic-disease therapies in pregnancy; ceasing it increases SLE flare risk and adverse pregnancy outcomes. (Source: ACR)
source_refs: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases

explanation.summary: Pregnancy in SLE is best planned during a period of disease quiescence. Hydroxychloroquine is continued throughout pregnancy because it reduces flares and is safe; low-dose prednisolone under 20 mg daily is acceptable. Low-dose aspirin from 12 weeks is recommended for pre-eclampsia prevention. Anti-Ro/La positive women need fetal cardiac monitoring for congenital heart block. Methotrexate and mycophenolate are teratogenic and must be ceased before conception.
key_points: Continue hydroxychloroquine; reduces flares and is safe.
Add low-dose aspirin from 12 weeks for pre-eclampsia prevention.
Anti-Ro/La positivity requires fetal echo surveillance.
explanation.pearls: In SLE, stopping hydroxychloroquine in pregnancy doubles the flare rate; counsel patients to continue, not stop.

sources.label: ACR Reproductive Health in Rheumatic Diseases

subtopic_detail: Macrosomia and mode of delivery in diabetes
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 38+2 weeks of pregnancy with insulin-treated gestational diabetes. Her growth scan today estimates the fetal weight at 4650 g with abdominal circumference greater than the 97th centile. She had a previous spontaneous vaginal birth at term of a 3700 g baby with no shoulder dystocia and no perineal trauma. She is otherwise well, the lie is longitudinal cephalic, and her bishop score is 4. She asks about mode of delivery.

data_table.Examination: Longitudinal cephalic, head 4/5 palpable, no contractions, no bleeding
data_table.Investigations: US EFW 4650 g (AC over 97th centile), normal liquor and Doppler, glucose log within target on insulin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Schedule symphysiotomy at the time of labour to manage the anticipated shoulder dystocia
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is not part of routine AU obstetric practice; planned caesarean is the preferred risk reduction for predicted significant macrosomia in diabetes. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Allow spontaneous onset of labour up to 41 weeks and plan a vaginal birth in delivery suite
options.rationale: Awaiting spontaneous labour beyond 39 weeks in insulin-treated GDM increases stillbirth and shoulder-dystocia risk; AU practice is delivery by 39-40 weeks. (Source: ADIPS)
source_refs: ADIPS GDM consensus and RANZCOG Delivery in diabetes

options.text: Offer elective caesarean at 39+0 weeks for any insulin-treated GDM regardless of fetal weight
options.rationale: Insulin-treated GDM alone, without macrosomia or other complication, does not mandate caesarean; mode of delivery is individualised. (Source: ADIPS)
source_refs: ADIPS GDM consensus and RANZCOG Delivery in diabetes

options.text: Offer planned caesarean section because of estimated fetal weight at least 4500 g in diabetes
options.rationale: RANZCOG and ADIPS guidance recommends offering caesarean delivery when estimated fetal weight is 4500 g or more in women with diabetes, given the increased risk of shoulder dystocia and brachial plexus injury. (Source: ADIPS GDM consensus)
source_refs: ADIPS GDM consensus and RANZCOG Delivery in diabetes

options.text: Offer induction of labour at 38+3 weeks of gestation aiming for assisted vaginal birth
options.rationale: Induction is appropriate for less extreme macrosomia or other indications, but at an EFW over 4500 g in diabetes the AU preference is to offer caesarean section because of shoulder dystocia risk. (Source: ADIPS)
source_refs: ADIPS GDM consensus and RANZCOG Delivery in diabetes

explanation.summary: Australian guidance recommends offering caesarean delivery when estimated fetal weight is 4500 g or more in pregnancies complicated by diabetes, and 5000 g or more in non-diabetic pregnancies, because of substantially increased shoulder dystocia and brachial plexus injury risk. Insulin-treated GDM alone is generally an indication for delivery by 39 to 40 weeks rather than awaiting spontaneous onset. Mode of delivery is otherwise individualised.
key_points: Offer caesarean at EFW 4500 g or more in diabetes.
Offer caesarean at EFW 5000 g or more in non-diabetic pregnancies.
Deliver insulin-treated GDM by 39 to 40 weeks.
explanation.pearls: Counselling for macrosomia in diabetes should include the 1 in 5 risk of shoulder dystocia at EFW over 4500 g and the 1 in 10 risk of brachial plexus injury within dystocia.

sources.label: ADIPS GDM consensus and RANZCOG Delivery in diabetes

subtopic_detail: DOAC choice in atrial fibrillation with CKD stage 3
stem: You are the GP seeing a 78-year-old woman with newly diagnosed paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Her CHA2DS2-VA score is 4 (age over 75, hypertension, prior TIA, female sex not counted in CHA2DS2-VA). Her HAS-BLED is 2. Her eGFR is 38 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD G3b) and serum creatinine 145 micromol/L. She has no liver disease, no recent bleeding and weighs 62 kg. She is hesitant about warfarin because she cannot easily attend frequent INR checks.

data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, no peripheral oedema, no murmurs, no neurology
data_table.Investigations: ECG AF, eGFR 38, Cr 145, Hb 128, LFTs normal, INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulant strategy?

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily titrated against renal function with trough drug sampling
options.rationale: Dabigatran is 80% renally cleared and is not preferred when eGFR is below 50; doses below 30 mL/min are contraindicated. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 with weekly point-of-care monitoring at her local clinic
options.rationale: Warfarin remains an option (and the only option in mechanical valves and moderate to severe mitral stenosis), but DOACs are preferred when not contraindicated, particularly when reliable INR monitoring is difficult. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Long-term aspirin 100 mg daily as monotherapy for stroke prophylaxis in atrial fibrillation
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF because it offers little stroke benefit at significant bleeding risk. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily, dose-reduce if two of age 80, weight 60 kg, Cr 133 micromol/L
options.rationale: Apixaban is preferred in CKD G3b because it has the lowest renal clearance of the available DOACs in AU; dose-reduction criteria (any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, creatinine over 133 micromol/L) drive the 2.5 mg twice daily dose. (Source: NHFA AF guideline 2023)

options.text: Defer anticoagulation until Holter confirms at least six hours per day of AF burden
options.rationale: Documented paroxysmal AF on ECG with a CHA2DS2-VA score above 1 already meets criteria for anticoagulation; no time-in-AF threshold is required. (Source: NHFA)

explanation.summary: In non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CKD stage 3, apixaban is the preferred DOAC because of its lower dependence on renal clearance compared with dabigatran or rivaroxaban. Apixaban dose reduction criteria are any two of age 80 or more, weight 60 kg or less, or creatinine 133 micromol/L or more. Aspirin alone is not recommended for stroke prevention. Warfarin remains a reasonable option, particularly in valvular AF, but DOACs are preferred when monitoring is difficult.
key_points: Apixaban first-line in CKD stage 3 non-valvular AF.
Dose reduce if two of age 80, weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 or more.
Aspirin alone is not appropriate stroke prevention in AF.
explanation.pearls: Recheck the apixaban dose-reduction criteria at each annual review; weight loss in older patients tips many into the 2.5 mg twice daily dose.

stem: You are the ED registrar seeing a 56-year-old man with severe occipital headache, blurred vision and nausea for 6 hours. His BP at triage is 222/128 mmHg. He has a background of long-standing untreated hypertension and is normally well. Fundoscopy shows bilateral flame haemorrhages and a single cotton wool spot. He has new mild confusion and his ECG shows LVH with strain. He has no chest pain, no neurological deficit and no recent stroke. The renal function is mildly impaired with creatinine 145 micromol/L.

data_table.Examination: Mildly drowsy, GCS 14, no lateralising signs, fundoscopy as above
data_table.Investigations: Cr 145 micromol/L, K 3.9, troponin negative, ECG LVH strain pattern, CT head no acute haemorrhage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate blood pressure target and agent in the next hour?

options.text: Discharge home on oral perindopril 5 mg with GP review in 48 hours of presentation
options.rationale: A hypertensive emergency with acute target organ damage cannot be managed as an outpatient; it requires monitored IV therapy. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Lower the mean arterial pressure by 20 to 25 percent in the first hour with IV labetalol
options.rationale: In hypertensive emergency with acute target-organ damage (encephalopathy, retinopathy, AKI), the standard is a controlled fall in MAP of 20 to 25% over the first hour using IV titratable agents in a monitored setting; abrupt larger reductions risk watershed ischaemia. (Source: Heart Foundation hypertension guideline 2024)

options.text: Defer pharmacological treatment until ambulatory monitoring confirms sustained elevation
options.rationale: Documented hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage warrants immediate treatment without further ambulatory monitoring. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Commence oral hydralazine plus oral clonidine simultaneously aiming for fast normotension
options.rationale: Oral combination therapy with hydralazine and clonidine is less controllable than IV titratable agents in a hypertensive emergency and can cause unpredictable BP swings. (Source: NHFA)

options.text: Bring BP to 140/90 mmHg within the first 30 minutes using sublingual nifedipine capsules
options.rationale: Rapid uncontrolled drops, particularly with sublingual nifedipine, are associated with watershed cerebral and renal infarction and are not recommended. (Source: NHFA)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is sustained BP elevation with acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, acute LV failure, dissection, eclampsia, retinopathy, AKI). It is managed with IV titratable agents (labetalol, hydralazine, GTN, clevidipine) in a monitored setting, with a controlled 20 to 25 percent reduction in MAP in the first hour to avoid watershed ischaemia. Hypertensive urgency without acute target organ damage is managed with reintroduction or up-titration of oral therapy in primary care or short-stay observation.
key_points: Reduce MAP 20-25% in the first hour, then more gradually.
Use IV titratable agents in a monitored bed.
Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated due to rapid swings.
explanation.pearls: In aortic dissection or pre-eclampsia the BP target is tighter and faster (systolic under 120 within minutes); for encephalopathy aim only for a 20-25% MAP reduction in the first hour.
